body.p:
DITAContent Model At this point, let us briefly look at the extensive content model of DITA in the form of XML snippets. Just enough DITA as is necessary to be able to work with Tektur CCMS.
UWE test footnote (12/40, callout L): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
UWE supplement (fn-add) for the footnote index.

dd.p:
Paragraphs allow the common inline elements, e.g. ,,,,, and are marked up using the

tag.
dd.pre: Das ist ein Para mit fettem und kursiven Text, sowie mit hochgestellter Quadratzahl und tiefgestellter Zahlenuntergrenze .

dd.p: Footnotes are marked up using the <fn> tag. The @callout attribute is optional and specifies the footnote key. Without specification, the footnote is usually numbered sequentially.
dd.pre: <fn callout="#1"> Das ist Text in einer Fußnote. Hier sind auch Links erlaubt <link href="http://www.tekturcms.de">Tektur Homepage</link> </fn>

dd.p: Links can point externally to a resource on the internet, but also internally to another topic. In that case, the syntax would look like this:
Index entries are marked up with the <indexterm> element. They can appear directly in the content, among other places, and in Tektur CCMS they can also have a nested sub-entry.
Inline icons can be specified using the <image> element.
Inline code snippets or file paths, etc. are set using the <codeph> element.
The graphic can be placed page-wide, column-wide, or in the margin. In Tektur CCMS this is controlled via the @expanse attribute.
Source code or preformatted text can either be specified inline via the <codeph> element or as a block element via the <pre> element.
dd.pre:
for i in range(10):
  print i

dd.p: Hazard statements are specified using the <hazardstatement> element. There are four different types:
This is controlled via the @type attribute.
dd.pre: <hazardstatement type="warning"> <messagepanel> <typeofhazard> Heisse Flächen sollte nicht berührt werden. </typeofhazard> <howtoavoid>Warnschilder anbringen</howtoavoid> <howtoavoid>Temperaturregler einschalten</howtoavoid> </messagepanel> </hazardstatement>
dd.p: The description of the hazard <typeofhazard> can be followed by multiple avoidance measures (<howtoavoid).
The simple note is specified using the <note> element.
dd.pre: Ohne weitere Maßnahmen werden Dokumente mit den Rechten des Erzeugers versehen.

dd.p: Ordered, unordered and definition lists are available. <ul> and <ol> lists can be set analogously to HTML, for example. Additionally, DITA also allows paragraphs in list items. The following examples are from the DITA documentation page DITA documentation for the <ul> element:
dd.pre:
1. Red
  2. Orange

  3. Yellow

  4. Green
  5. Blue
  6. Indigo
  7. Violet
  • This is an item in an unordered list.
  • To separate it from other items in the list, the formatter puts a bullet beside it.
  • The following paragraph, contained in the list item element, is part of the list item which contains it.

    This is the contained paragraph.

  • This is the last list item in our unordered list.
dd.p: Additionally, the definition list <dl> is available, which is also used to realize this chapter:
dd.pre: <dl> <dlhead> <dthd>Image File View Selection</dthd> <ddhd>Resulting Information</ddhd> </dlhead> <dlentry> <dt>File Type</dt> <dd>Image's file extension</dd> </dlentry> <dlentry> <dt>Image Class</dt> <dd>Image is raster, vector, metafile or 3D</dd> </dlentry> <dlentry> <dt>Number of pages</dt> <dd>Number of pages in the image</dd> </dlentry> <dlentry> <dt>Fonts</dt> <dd>Fonts contained within a vector image</dd> </dlentry> </dl>

dd.p: A topic should form a self-contained information unit that can be embedded in various contexts.
dd.pre:
Der Titel des Topics

Ein einleitender Satz vor der Grafik

Ein abschliessender Satz zum Topic

dd.p: A section is a subsection with a subheading within a topic.
dd.pre:
Unterabschnitt

Blockelemente, wie Paras, folgen direkt dem Titel

dd.p: The chapter structure in DITA is defined using a nested topic structure, the so-called DITA Map. Here, <topicref> elements are used that point to existing topics via the @href attribute. The titles of the individual topics are stored in the @navtitle attribute for generating the table of contents.
dd.pre: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?> <map title="XML Entwicklerhandbuch"> <topicmeta> <navtitle>XSLT - XQuery - MarkLogic</navtitle> <shortdesc/> <keywords> <keyword>XML</keyword> <keyword>XSLT</keyword> <keyword>XQUERY</keyword> </keywords> </topicmeta> <topicref href="1_Intro.dita" navtitle="Intro"/> <topicref href="2_Application_Areas.dita" navtitle="Anwendungsgebiete"> <topicref href="2_1_XSLT_Programming_Language_in_XML_Domain.dita" navtitle="XSLT - die Programmiersprache im XML Bereich"/> <topicref href="2_2_Current_and_Past_Applications.dita" navtitle="Aktuelle und vergangene Anwendungen"/> [...]

section.title: Task Elements
section.p:
The task
element describes a procedure or set of instructions. Since it is very complex, a separate section is reserved for it in this book.

A well-populated task with one child element each is shown below as an XML snippet. Of course, at certain positions within the elements, additional block elements such as hazard statements, lists, tables, etc. are also permitted.
section.pre:
Ein Task

Abstract-Element in Task

Prereq-Element in Task

Context-Element in Task

Stepsection-Element in Task

 Cmd-Element in Step Substep-Element in Step

Info-Element in Substep

Stepresult-Element in Substep

Choice-Element in Step

Zweites Choice-Element in Step

Choicetable mit leeren Zellen in Step

Info-Element in Step

Stepresult-Element in Step

Result-Element in Task

Postreq-Element in task
section.p: From this XML snippet, you can easily have the underlying grammar generated as a graphic, e.g. with the oXygen XML Editor. (Since it is an SVG graphic, you should be able to zoom in losslessly on a computer)

dd.p: The <abstract> element can contain further block elements and serves for the initial description of the instruction, process or procedure (I hope I have listed all synonyms relevant in this context)

In the body of the task taskbody, prerequisites can be specified that must be met before the instruction can be executed. In this prereq element, further block elements such as lists and tables are again permitted. Of course, in the output, this section should be marked in some way - like all other elements - to distinguish it from the body text.
In the <context> element, the context (environment, purpose and benefit) in which the procedure is to be executed is described. Here, too, many block elements are allowed.
Finally, in the <steps> element you also have the option to enter additional chapter content - via the <stepsection> element.
The <step> element itself has, in the simplest case, a <substeps> element, so that a simple nested numbered list can be represented.
dd.pre:
Cmd-Element in Step Substep-Element in Step

Info-Element in Substep

Stepresult-Element in Substep

 [...]
dd.p: The <cmd> element contains the action to be performed, while other elements further specify the action. Particularly interesting is that hazard statements (<hazardstatement>) must always be placed before the <cmd> element. The <info> element can contain additional information. The <stepresult> finally documents the result of the step.

If the person executing the step has multiple options for the action, this can be modeled using the <choices> element. Instead of a list, a tabular representation (<choicetable>) is also available, which in addition to the action (<choption>) also allows a description (<chdesc>) of the same.
dd.pre:
Choice-Element in Step

Zweites Choice-Element in Step

Choicetable mit leeren Zellen in Step

dd.p: When all steps are executed, a result (<result>) should be documented. These result elements are also available in the <step> and <substep> elements as the <stepresult> element.
Analogous to the task prerequisite, a postcondition can also be documented via the <postreq> element.
